questions.stem: What does HTML stand for, and what is its primary purpose?

options.A: Hypertext Markup Language — defines the structure and meaning of web content using elements and tags
options.B: High-Level Transfer Markup Language — transfers data between servers
options.C: Hypertext Management Language — manages web server configurations
options.D: Hypermedia Text Markup Language — styles the visual appearance of web pages

questions.explanation:
HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for structuring web content. It uses elements represented by tags (e.g.,

,

, ) to give meaning to content — defining headings, paragraphs, links, images, and more. HTML is responsible for structure and semantics; CSS handles presentation; JavaScript handles behaviour.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Introduction to HTML. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML; W3C. HTML Living Standard. https://html.spec.whatwg.org

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct basic structure of an HTML document?

options.A: <html><title>Page</title><body>Content</body></html>
options.B: <!DOCTYPE html><html lang='en'><head><meta charset='UTF-8'><title>Page</title></head><body>Content</body></html>
options.C: <document><head><title>Page</title></head><main>Content</main></document>
options.D: <webpage><header>Page</header><content>Content</content></webpage>

questions.explanation: A valid HTML document requires: (1) <!DOCTYPE html> — declares HTML5; (2) <html lang='en'> — root element with language attribute; (3) <head> — metadata container with <meta charset='UTF-8'> and <title>; (4) <body> — visible page content. The <!DOCTYPE> declaration prevents browsers from entering quirks mode, which can cause unexpected rendering differences.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Anatomy of an HTML document. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Getting_started

questions.stem: Why is it important to include <!DOCTYPE html> at the very start of an HTML file?

options.A: It tells the browser which CSS stylesheet to load
options.B: It instructs the browser to render the page using standards mode (HTML5) rather than quirks mode — preventing inconsistent legacy rendering behaviour across different browsers
options.C: It is optional and has no effect on modern browsers
options.D: It defines the character encoding of the document

questions.explanation: Browsers have two rendering modes: standards mode (predictable, spec-compliant) and quirks mode (emulates bugs from the 1990s for legacy compatibility). Without <!DOCTYPE html>, browsers may enter quirks mode, causing layout inconsistencies — for example, different box model behaviour in IE. <!DOCTYPE html> is the shortest valid doctype and triggers standards mode in all modern browsers.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Quirks mode and standards mode. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Quirks_Mode_and_Standards_Mode

questions.stem: What is the difference between the <head> and <body> elements in an HTML document?

options.A: <head> contains the visible page title; <body> contains navigation links only
options.B: <head> contains metadata about the document (title, charset, linked stylesheets, scripts) that is not directly visible on the page; <body> contains all content that is rendered and visible to users in the browser viewport
options.C: <head> and <body> are interchangeable — content can go in either
options.D: <body> contains SEO metadata; <head> contains the main page content

questions.explanation: <head> metadata: <title> (browser tab and SEO), <meta charset>, <meta name='viewport'>, <link rel='stylesheet'>, <script defer>. None of this is directly rendered in the page. <body> contains everything the user sees and interacts with: headings, paragraphs, images, forms, navigation. This separation keeps document metadata cleanly separate from content.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. What's in the head? Metadata in HTML. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/The_head_metadata_in_HTML

questions.stem: You are creating a webpage about your favourite book. Write the minimal valid HTML5 skeleton with a meaningful page title and a visible heading that says 'My Favourite Book'. Which option is correct?

options.A: My Favourite Book
options.B: My Favourite Book
options.C:
My Favourite Book

My Favourite Book
options.D: My Favourite Book

questions.explanation: Option B is the only fully valid HTML5 document: <!DOCTYPE html> triggers standards mode; <html lang='en'> declares language for accessibility and SEO; <meta charset='UTF-8'> prevents character encoding issues; <title> in <head> sets the browser tab name; <h1> in <body> is the visible rendered heading. All other options are missing required structural elements.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Getting started with HTML. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Getting_started

questions.stem: HTML provides six heading levels. Which element represents the most important (highest-level) heading, and which represents the least important?

options.A: <h6> is the most important; <h1> is the least important
options.B: <h1> is the most important (largest, highest in hierarchy); <h6> is the least important (smallest, lowest in hierarchy)
options.C: <heading> is the most important; <subheading> is the least important
options.D: All heading levels are equally important — only size differs

questions.explanation: HTML headings <h1> through <h6> create a document outline. <h1> is the top-level heading — there should be exactly one per page for SEO and accessibility (screen readers use it to announce the page topic). <h2> introduces major sections; <h3> subsections within those, and so on down to <h6>. Never skip heading levels (e.g., <h1> → <h3>) as this breaks the document outline for assistive technologies.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML text fundamentals. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/HTML_text_fundamentals

questions.stem: Which HTML element creates an unordered (bulleted) list, and which creates an ordered (numbered) list?

options.A: <ul> for ordered; <ol> for unordered
options.B: <ul> (unordered list) for bulleted lists; <ol> (ordered list) for numbered lists — both use <li> (list item) elements as children
options.C: <list> for unordered; <numlist> for ordered
options.D: <bl> for bulleted; <nl> for numbered

questions.explanation: <ul>: unordered list — rendered as bullet points by default. Use for items with no meaningful sequence (shopping list, navigation links). <ol>: ordered list — rendered as numbers by default. Use when sequence matters (recipe steps, ranked results). Both require <li> children. Nested lists are created by placing a <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> element.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML text fundamentals — Lists. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/HTML_text_fundamentals#Lists

questions.stem: What is the semantic difference between <strong> and <b>, and between <em> and <i> in HTML?

options.A: They are completely identical — <strong>/<b> and <em>/<i> are interchangeable
options.B: <strong> conveys semantic importance (screen readers may stress it); <b> is purely stylistic bold with no semantic meaning. <em> conveys emphasis that changes meaning (screen readers may stress it); <i> is stylistic italic used for technical terms, foreign words, or titles — no emphasis meaning
options.C: <b> conveys importance; <strong> is purely decorative
options.D: <em> makes text bold; <i> makes text italic

questions.explanation: Semantic elements carry meaning beyond appearance. <strong>: the content is of strong importance — assistive technologies may convey this. <b>: draw visual attention without implying importance (e.g., keywords in a review). <em>: stress emphasis that changes sentence meaning ('I did NOT say he stole it'). <i>: idiomatic text, technical terms, foreign phrases, fictional character thoughts. Use semantic elements whenever meaning is intended.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Advanced text formatting. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Advanced_text_formatting

questions.stem:
When should you use a

element versus a
element to separate text content?

options.A:
and
are interchangeable for creating visual space between text
options.B:
wraps a distinct paragraph of content — a thematic unit of text with semantic meaning, margin above and below.
creates a single line break within flowing text — appropriate for addresses, poetry, or lyrics where line breaks are part of the content, not a new paragraph
options.C:
should be used for paragraphs;

should only be used for headings
options.D:
Multiple
tags are the correct way to add space between sections

questions.explanation:
is a block-level element that groups a thematic unit of text — browsers add default top and bottom margins. Use for body text paragraphs.
is an inline void element that forces a line break within the same flow — used inside

for addresses (123 Main St
London) or verse. Anti-pattern: using multiple

for spacing — use CSS margin/padding instead.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML text fundamentals. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/HTML_text_fundamentals

questions.stem: Mark up a recipe introduction with: a main heading 'Classic Pancakes', a paragraph describing the recipe, and an ordered list of three steps: 'Mix ingredients', 'Heat pan', 'Cook until golden'. Which HTML is correct?

options.A:
Classic Pancakes

A simple recipe.

  • Mix ingredients
  • Heat pan
  • Cook until golden
options.B:
Classic Pancakes

A simple recipe.

  1. Mix ingredients
  2. Heat pan
  3. Cook until golden
options.C:
A simple recipe.

  1. Mix ingredients
  2. Heat pan
  3. Cook until golden
options.D:
Classic Pancakes

A simple recipe.

Mix ingredientsHeat panCook until golden

questions.explanation:
Option B is correct:

for the main heading;

for the introductory paragraph;

    for the ordered (numbered) steps because recipe steps have a meaningful sequence — step 2 must follow step 1. Option A uses
      (unordered) which does not convey sequence. Option C uses which belongs in <head>, not <body>. Option D uses non-existent <list> and <item> elements.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML text fundamentals. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/HTML_text_fundamentals

questions.stem: What is an HTML attribute, and give an example of an attribute on an anchor element?

options.A: An attribute is a CSS property applied to an HTML element
options.B: An attribute provides additional information about an HTML element, written as name='value' pairs inside the opening tag. Example: Link — href is the attribute name, the URL is its value
options.C: An attribute is a JavaScript function attached to an element
options.D: An attribute is the visible text content inside an HTML element

questions.explanation: HTML attributes: written in the opening tag as name='value'. Common attributes: href (link destination), src (image source), alt (image alternative text), class (CSS selector), id (unique identifier), type (input type). Boolean attributes have no value: <input disabled>. Attribute values should always be quoted (single or double quotes — consistent throughout the document).
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML attributes. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Getting_started#attributes

questions.stem: Which attribute is mandatory on every element, and why?

options.A: The title attribute — provides a tooltip on hover
options.B: The alt attribute — provides alternative text that screen readers announce when the image cannot be seen, and browsers display when the image fails to load
options.C: The width attribute — controls the display size of the image
options.D: The class attribute — required for CSS styling of images

questions.explanation: alt is mandatory for accessibility (WCAG 2.1 criterion 1.1.1). Rules: descriptive alt for content images (alt='A golden retriever puppy playing in the park'); empty alt='' for decorative images (screen readers skip them); never omit alt entirely (screen readers announce the filename instead). — also always include width and height to prevent layout shift (CLS).
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Images in HTML. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Images_in_HTML; WCAG 2.1. Success Criterion 1.1.1. https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/quickref/#non-text-content

questions.stem: What is the difference between an absolute URL and a relative URL in an href attribute, and when should each be used?

options.A: Absolute URLs are for images; relative URLs are for links
options.B: Absolute URL: full address including protocol and domain (https://example.com/page) — use for linking to external websites. Relative URL: path relative to the current file (/about or ../images/photo.jpg) — use for linking to pages and assets within the same website, as it works regardless of domain name
options.C: Relative URLs always start with http://; absolute URLs start with /
options.D: Both types are identical — browsers treat them the same way

questions.explanation: Absolute URL: https://www.example.com/contact — always works from any context but brittle during development (domain changes break links). Relative URL: contact.html or ../contact.html — relative to the current document's location. Root-relative URL: /contact.html — relative to the website root, works from any page. Best practice: root-relative URLs for internal navigation; absolute URLs for external links.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Creating hyperlinks. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Creating_hyperlinks

questions.stem: Why should external links in HTML include the attribute target='_blank' rel='noopener noreferrer'?

options.A: target='_blank' makes the link open in a new tab; rel='noopener noreferrer' prevents the new tab from accessing the original page via window.opener — closing a security vulnerability and protecting user privacy by not sending the referrer URL
options.B: target='_blank' applies bold styling; rel='noopener' adds an underline
options.C: Both attributes are required for links to load faster
options.D: rel='noopener' is only needed for image links, not text links

questions.explanation: target='_blank': opens link in new tab. Security risk without rel='noopener': the opened page can access window.opener and redirect the original page (reverse tabnapping attack). rel='noopener': severs the opener relationship. rel='noreferrer': additionally prevents sending the Referer HTTP header — protecting the user's navigation history from the destination site. Always combine both for external _blank links.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Creating hyperlinks — Security. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Creating_hyperlinks#e-mail_links

questions.stem: Write the correct HTML to display an image of a cat (file: cat.jpg, in an 'images' subfolder) with descriptive alt text, a width of 400px, and wrap it in a link to https://www.cats.com that opens in a new tab safely.

options.C: <link href='https://www.cats.com'><image src='images/cat.jpg' alt='cat'></link>

questions.explanation: Option B is correct: rel='noopener noreferrer' with target='_blank' for safe external link; src='images/cat.jpg' uses relative path to the subfolder; alt text is descriptive (not 'cat' or empty); width and height specified to prevent layout shift; loading='lazy' defers off-screen loading. Option A: missing alt, rel, target. Option C: <link> and <image> are incorrect elements. Option D: target='new' creates a named window, not a new tab.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Images in HTML. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Images_in_HTML

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · Semantic HTML and Accessibility Basics

questions.stem: What is semantic HTML?

options.A: HTML that uses only <div> and <span> elements for all page structure
options.B: HTML that uses elements whose names describe the meaning and role of the content they contain — such as <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, and <footer> — rather than generic containers
options.C: HTML that includes inline CSS styling on every element
options.D: HTML that is written with a specific font and colour scheme

questions.explanation: Semantic elements communicate meaning to browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies. <header>: introductory content or navigation. <nav>: navigation links. <main>: primary content (one per page). <article>: self-contained content (blog post, news item). <section>: thematic grouping. <aside>: tangentially related content. <footer>: footer. Contrast with <div> (no meaning) and <span> (no meaning). Semantic HTML improves SEO, accessibility, and maintainability.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. HTML layout elements in more detail. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Document_and_website_structure

questions.stem: Which HTML element should wrap the primary, unique content of a webpage — and how many times should it appear per page?

options.A: <section> — can appear multiple times per page
options.B: <main> — exactly once per page, containing the dominant content unique to that page, excluding repeated elements like headers and footers
options.C: <div id='main'> — the id attribute makes any div the main element
options.D: <body> — contains all content so it serves as the main element

questions.explanation: <main> identifies the dominant content of the <body> — unique to the page, not repeated across pages. ARIA landmark: screen readers can jump directly to <main> via keyboard shortcut, skipping repeated navigation. Only one <main> per page (unless others have hidden attribute). The 'skip to main content' accessibility link targets <main>. Common mistake: wrapping everything in <main> — keep <header>, <nav>, <footer> outside it.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. <main>. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/main; WCAG 2.1. Bypass Blocks (2.4.1). https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/quickref/#bypass-blocks

questions.stem: Why does using semantic HTML elements like <nav>, <main>, and <article> benefit users who rely on screen readers?

options.A: Semantic elements make text larger, which helps visually impaired users
options.B: Semantic elements create ARIA landmark regions that screen readers expose to users — allowing them to navigate directly to the header, navigation, main content, or footer by keyboard shortcut, rather than reading through the entire page linearly
options.C: Screen readers only work with websites that use semantic HTML exclusively
options.D: Semantic elements are needed only for mobile accessibility, not desktop

questions.explanation: Screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) map semantic HTML to ARIA landmarks: <header>→banner, <nav>→navigation, <main>→main, <footer>→contentinfo, <aside>→complementary. Users can pull up a landmarks list and jump instantly to any region — critical for productivity. A page using only <div> elements has no landmarks — users must arrow through every element to find content. This is why semantic HTML is a WCAG 2.1 requirement.
questions.source: WebAIM. Semantic Structure: Regions, Headings, and Lists. https://webaim.org/techniques/semanticstructure; WCAG 2.1. Info and Relationships (1.3.1).

questions.stem: What is the difference between <section> and <article> in HTML5?

options.A: <section> and <article> are identical — use either for any grouped content
options.B: <article> represents self-contained, independently distributable content (a blog post, news story, product card) that makes sense on its own. <section> represents a thematic grouping of content within a larger document — it requires surrounding context to make sense
options.C: <section> is for text content; <article> is for images only
options.D: <article> must always contain a <header>; <section> cannot

questions.explanation: Litmus test for <article>: 'Would this content still make sense if extracted and syndicated in an RSS feed or a different page?' If yes → <article>. Examples: blog post, news article, forum post, product review. <section>: 'About Us', 'Our Services', 'Chapter 3' — meaningful only in context of the surrounding page. Articles can contain sections; sections can contain articles. Both should typically have a heading.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. <article>. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/article; HTML Living Standard. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/sections.html

questions.stem:
Refactor this non-semantic HTML into semantic HTML:
...
...
...

options.A:
...
...
...
options.B: <header><nav>...</nav></header><main><article>...</article></main><footer>...</footer>
options.C:
...
...
...
options.D: <top><links>...</links></top><content><post>...</post></content><bottom>...</bottom>

questions.explanation: Option B is the most semantically correct: <header> replaces <div id='header'>; <nav> inside it (was already semantic but wrapped in a div); <main> replaces <div id='main'> (id-based targeting is not semantic); <article> replaces <div class='post'> because a blog post is self-contained distributable content; <footer> replaces <div id='footer'>. Option A is also valid but uses a generic <div> for the post — B is more precise. Option C uses div with class — still non-semantic.
questions.source: MDN Web Docs. Document and website structure. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Document_and_website_structure

table_of_contents.title: MQ-4 · Semantic HTML and Accessibility Basics
